title: Emperor Penguin Overview
text: The Emperor penguin is the tallest and heaviest of all living penguin species and is endemic to Antarctica. The male and female are similar in plumage and size, reaching 100 cm in length and weighing from 22 to 45 kg. Their diet consists primarily of fish, but includes crustaceans, such as krill, and cephalopods, such as squid.

title: King Penguin Characteristics
text: The King penguin is the second largest species of penguin, smaller, but somewhat similar in appearance to the emperor penguin. They breed on the subantarctic islands at the northern reaches of Antarctica, South Georgia, and other temperate islands of the region. King penguins eat small fish, mainly lanternfish, and squid. They can dive to over 300 meters deep.

title: Adélie Penguin Habitat
text: The Adélie penguin is a species of penguin common along the entire coast of the Antarctic continent, which is its only habitat. They are among the most southerly distributed of all seabirds. They are named after Adélie Land, which in turn was named for the wife of French explorer Jules Dumont d'Urville. They feed mainly on Antarctic krill.

title: Chinstrap Penguin Appearance
text: The Chinstrap penguin inhabits a variety of islands and shores in the Southern Pacific and the Antarctic Oceans. Its name stems from the narrow black band under its head, which makes it appear as if it were wearing a black helmet, making it one of the most easily identified types of penguin. Their diet consists mainly of krill and small fish.

title: Gentoo Penguin Speed
text: The Gentoo penguin is easily recognized by the wide white stripe extending like a bonnet across the top of its head and its bright orange-red bill. They are the fastest underwater swimming penguins, reaching speeds of up to 36 kilometers per hour. They build nests from circular piles of stones and aggressively defend them from other penguins.

title: Macaroni Penguin Crest
text: The Macaroni penguin is a species of penguin found from the Subantarctic to the Antarctic Peninsula. One of six species of crested penguin, it bears a distinctive yellow crest, and the face and upperparts are black and sharply delineated from the white underparts. They are highly social animals, gathering in massive colonies to breed and molt.

title: Royal Penguin Endemism
text: The Royal penguin is a species of penguin, which can be found on the sub-Antarctic Macquarie Island and adjacent islands. They are very similar in appearance to the macaroni penguin. They inhabit the waters surrounding Antarctica. Royal penguins are migratory birds, leaving Macquarie Island after the breeding season, though their non-breeding winter distribution is largely unknown to researchers.

title: Fiordland Crested Penguin
text: The Fiordland penguin is a crested penguin species endemic to New Zealand. It currently breeds along the south-western coasts of South Island’s Fiordland and Stewart Island. It is a medium-sized penguin with a dark, bluish-grey upper part and a white belly. It features a prominent yellow crest over the eyes. They prefer to nest in dense coastal rainforests.

title: Snares Penguin Distribution
text: The Snares penguin is a penguin from New Zealand. The species breeds on the Snares Islands, a group of islands off the southern coast of the South Island. This is a medium-small, yellow-crested penguin. It has dark blue-black upperparts and white underparts. The main prey of the Snares penguin consists of krill, supplemented by small fish and cephalopods.

title: Erect-crested Penguin
text: The Erect-crested penguin is a penguin endemic to the New Zealand region. It breeds on the Bounty and Antipodes Islands. It has black upperparts, white underparts, and a yellow eye stripe and crest that stands erect. This species is poorly studied, but it is known that their populations have declined significantly over the past few decades due to unknown reasons.

title: Southern Rockhopper Behavior
text: The Southern Rockhopper penguin is a species of penguin known for its distinctive red eyes, orange beak, and the striking yellow crests of feathers that sweep back from its eyes. They are among the smallest of the world's penguins. True to their name, they often navigate their rocky coastal habitats by hopping from boulder to boulder rather than sliding.

title: Northern Rockhopper Penguin
text: The Northern Rockhopper penguin is a species of crested penguin. It is very similar to the southern rockhopper penguin, but it is generally larger and has a longer crest. They breed in cool temperate climates, including on the Tristan da Cunha and Gough Island. They are highly aggressive and will violently defend their nesting territories from intruders.

title: Little Blue Penguin Size
text: The Little penguin, also known as the little blue penguin or fairy penguin, is the smallest species of penguin. It grows to an average of 33 centimeters in height and 43 centimeters in length. They are found on the coastlines of southern Australia and New Zealand. Their blue plumage provides excellent camouflage from predators while swimming in the ocean.

title: White-flippered Penguin
text: The White-flippered penguin is a small penguin endemic to Canterbury, New Zealand. Some scientists consider it a subspecies of the little penguin, while others consider it a distinct species. It has white margins on its flippers. They nest in burrows, caves, and rock crevices, and they only come ashore under the cover of darkness to avoid predators like seabirds.

title: Magellanic Penguin Migration
text: The Magellanic penguin is a South American penguin, breeding in coastal Patagonia, including Argentina, Chile, and the Falkland Islands. They migrate north as far as Brazil during the winter months. They are medium-sized penguins with a black back and a white abdomen. They are known for their loud, braying calls that sound very much like a donkey.

title: Humboldt Penguin Climate
text: The Humboldt penguin is a South American penguin that breeds in coastal Chile and Peru. Its nearest relatives are the Galápagos penguin and the Magellanic penguin. They are named after the cold water current it swims in, which is named after Alexander von Humboldt. They face severe threats from climate change, El Niño events, and accidental capture in fishing nets.

title: Galapagos Penguin Heat
text: The Galapagos penguin is a penguin endemic to the Galapagos Islands, Ecuador. It is the only penguin found north of the equator. The cool waters of the Humboldt and Cromwell Currents allow it to survive despite the tropical latitude. To cool off, they pant like dogs and stand with their flippers extended to release heat from their bodies.

title: African Penguin Calls
text: The African penguin is a species of penguin confined to southern African waters. Like all extant penguins, it is flightless, with a streamlined body and wings stiffened and flattened into flippers for a marine habitat. They were formerly known as jackass penguins because of their loud, donkey-like braying, although several related species of South American penguins produce the same sound.

title: Penguin Diet and Hunting
text: Most penguins feed on krill, fish, and squid, caught while swimming underwater. They spend half of their lives on land and the other half in the ocean. Their spiky tongues and powerful jaws help them grip slippery prey. When hunting, they rely heavily on their excellent underwater vision, which allows them to spot prey even in deep, dark waters.

title: Penguin Predators
text: While penguins are agile predators in the water, they are also prey for other marine animals. Leopard seals and killer whales are their primary predators in the ocean. On land or ice, adult penguins have few natural enemies, but eggs and chicks are extremely vulnerable to predatory birds like skuas and giant petrels, which snatch unguarded young from nests.
